𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Nine — Part Eight

The Book of Tehillim

TEHILLIM

The Psalms — The Praises of Yisra'EL

Psalms 120 through 150

Book V Complete — The Songs of Ascents and the Five Hallelujah Psalms

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

11Q5 (11QPsa) — Cave 11 — Major Psalms Scroll — primary source

4Q84 (4QPsb) — Cave 4 — fragments

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — All 150 Psalms plus Psalm 151

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

BOOK V — PSALMS 120 THROUGH 150 — COMPLETE

THE SONGS OF ASCENTS — PSALMS 120 THROUGH 134

TEHILLIM — PSALM 120 A Song of Ascents — In My Distress I Called to YHWH — The Songs of Ascents Begin

1 In my distress I called to YHWH, and he answered me.

2 Deliver my soul, O YHWH, from lying lips, from a deceitful tongue.

3 What shall be given to you, and what more shall be done to you, you deceitful tongue?

4 A warrior's sharp arrows, with glowing coals of the broom tree.

5 Woe to me, that I sojourn in Meshekh, that I dwell among the tents of Kedar.

6 Too long have I had my dwelling among those who hate peace.

7 I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 120 opens the Songs of Ascents — Psalms 120-134 — a collection of fifteen pilgrimage psalms sung as worshipers ascended to Yerushalaym for the three great festivals. It is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 120 in full. The Songs of Ascents are central to the Beta Israel pilgrimage tradition — psalms of the covenant community journeying toward the presence of YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 120. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 120 opens the Songs of Ascents with a cry from the farthest point of exile — Meshekh in the north, Kedar in the south — the psalmist dwelling among those who hate peace. Verse 7 — I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war. The covenant person's orientation is shalom — wholeness and peace — but the world around him is oriented toward conflict. The Songs of Ascents as a whole are the journey from this condition of exile toward YHWH's presence in Tziyon.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 121 A Song of Ascents — I Lift Up My Eyes to the Hills — He Who Keeps Yisra'EL Will Not Slumber

1 I lift up my eyes to the hills. From where does my help come?

2 My help comes from YHWH, who made heaven and earth.

3 He will not let your foot be moved — he who keeps you will not slumber.

4 Behold, he who keeps Yisra'EL will neither slumber nor sleep.

5 YHWH is your keeper — YHWH is your shade on your right hand.

6 The sun shall not strike you by day, nor the moon by night.

7 YHWH will keep you from all evil — he will keep your life.

8 YHWH will keep your going out and your coming in from this time forth and forevermore.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 121 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is structured as a dialogue — a question and a sustained answer. The word keep or keeper appears six times in eight verses. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 121 in full. Psalm 121 is one of the most beloved psalms in the Beta Israel tradition — the declaration that YHWH neither slumbers nor sleeps as the keeper of his covenant people.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 121. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — I lift up my eyes to the hills. From where does my help come? The question is answered immediately in verse 2: from YHWH who made heaven and earth — not from the hills themselves. The hills are the pilgrimage road to Tziyon — but the help is not the destination, it is the Creator of both the hills and the pilgrim. The word shamar — keep — sounds six times in verses 3-8. YHWH keeps the foot from moving, keeps the life, keeps the going out and coming in. The pilgrim journeying toward Tziyon is kept at every step by the one who does not sleep.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 122 A Song of Ascents — Of Dawid — I Was Glad When They Said to Me — Pray for the Peace of Yerushalaym

1 I was glad when they said to me, Let us go to the house of YHWH.

2 Our feet have been standing within your gates, O Yerushalaym.

3 Yerushalaym — built as a city that is bound firmly together,

4 To which the tribes go up, the tribes of YHWH, as was decreed for Yisra'EL, to give thanks to the name of YHWH.

5 There thrones for judgment were set, the thrones of the house of Dawid.

6 Pray for the peace of Yerushalaym — may they be secure who love you.

7 Peace be within your walls and security within your towers.

8 For my brothers and companions' sake I will say, Peace be within you.

9 For the sake of the house of YHWH our Creator, I will seek your good.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 122 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a pilgrimage arrival psalm — the pilgrim has arrived in Yerushalaym and speaks of the city with joy and covenant purpose. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 122 in full. The prayer for the peace of Yerushalaym in verse 6 — shalu shalom Yerushalayim — is one of the most recognized covenant prayers in the Beta Israel tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 122. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — pray for the peace of Yerushalaym. The Hebrew shalu shalom — ask for shalom — uses a word related to the city's name: Yerushalaym — the city of shalom. The city's name carries within it the prayer for its wellbeing. Verse 9 — for the sake of the house of YHWH our Creator, I will seek your good. The pilgrim's love for Yerushalaym is not civic pride but covenant orientation: the house of YHWH is there. The city's good is sought because YHWH dwells in it.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 123 A Song of Ascents — To You I Lift Up My Eyes — Have Mercy on Us

1 To you I lift up my eyes, O you who are enthroned in the heavens.

2 Behold, as the eyes of servants look to the hand of their master, as the eyes of a maidservant to the hand of her mistress, so our eyes look to YHWH our Creator, till he has mercy upon us.

3 Have mercy upon us, O YHWH, have mercy upon us, for we have had more than enough of contempt.

4 Our soul has had more than enough of the scorn of those who are at ease, of the contempt of the proud.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 123 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a brief communal lament — the covenant community looking to YHWH as servants look to their master, waiting for mercy. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 123 in full. The servant-master image of verse 2 is read as the defining posture of covenant dependence: the servant's eyes are fixed entirely on the master's hand, waiting for the signal of provision or direction.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 123. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — as the eyes of servants look to the hand of their master — so our eyes look to YHWH our Creator, till he has mercy upon us. The image is sustained and complete: the servant does not look to the master's face for approval, nor to the master's words for instruction in this moment — the eyes are fixed on the hand. The hand signals provision, direction, and protection. The covenant community's entire orientation is toward YHWH's hand — waiting, watching, dependent. The contempt of the proud — verse 4 — is the background pressure that makes this posture of dependent waiting an act of covenant faithfulness.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 124 A Song of Ascents — Of Dawid — If It Had Not Been YHWH Who Was on Our Side

1 If it had not been YHWH who was on our side — let Yisra'EL now say —

2 If it had not been YHWH who was on our side when people rose up against us,

3 Then they would have swallowed us up alive, when their anger was kindled against us.

4 Then the flood would have swept us away, the torrent would have gone over us.

5 Then over us would have gone the raging waters.

6 Blessed be YHWH, who has not given us as prey to their teeth.

7 We have escaped like a bird from the snare of the fowlers — the snare is broken, and we have escaped.

8 Our help is in the name of YHWH, who made heaven and earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 124 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a communal thanksgiving — the entire congregation meditating together on what would have happened if YHWH had not intervened. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 124 in full. The repeated conditional — if it had not been YHWH — is read as the covenant community's way of rehearsing their utter dependence on YHWH's active involvement in their survival.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 124. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — our help is in the name of YHWH, who made heaven and earth. The identical closing as Psalm 121:2 — the Creator of heaven and earth is the helper. The Songs of Ascents repeatedly return to this foundational declaration. Verses 3-5 pile up the images of what would have been — swallowed alive, swept away in flood, raging waters — to make the deliverance feel as visceral as the danger. Verse 7 — we have escaped like a bird from the snare. The snare is broken. The escape is real. YHWH broke what held the covenant people.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 125 A Song of Ascents — Those Who Trust in YHWH Are Like Mount Tziyon

1 Those who trust in YHWH are like Mount Tziyon, which cannot be moved, but abides forever.

2 As the mountains surround Yerushalaym, so YHWH surrounds his people, from this time forth and forevermore.

3 For the scepter of wickedness shall not rest on the land allotted to the righteous, lest the righteous stretch out their hands to do wrong.

4 Do good, O YHWH, to those who are good, and to those who are upright in their hearts.

5 But those who turn aside to their crooked ways YHWH will lead away with evildoers. Peace be upon Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 125 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm uses the geography of Yerushalaym — mountains surrounding the city — as the image of YHWH's protection surrounding his people. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 125 in full. Verse 2 — as the mountains surround Yerushalaym, so YHWH surrounds his people — is read as the covenant declaration that YHWH's protection is geographical, complete, and permanent.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 125. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — those who trust in YHWH are like Mount Tziyon, which cannot be moved, but abides forever. The stability of the covenant person is grounded not in their own faithfulness but in the object of their trust: YHWH himself, as immovable as Tziyon. Verse 2 — as the mountains surround Yerushalaym, so YHWH surrounds his people. The surrounding mountains are not a wall that keeps things in — they are a protective enclosure that keeps enemies out. YHWH's surrounding of his people is the same: he is the terrain itself that protects.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 126 A Song of Ascents — When YHWH Restored the Fortunes of Tziyon — Those Who Sow in Tears

1 When YHWH restored the fortunes of Tziyon, we were like those who dream.

2 Then our mouth was filled with laughter, and our tongue with shouts of joy — then they said among the nations, YHWH has done great things for them.

3 YHWH has done great things for us — we are glad.

4 Restore our fortunes, O YHWH, like streams in the Negev.

5 Those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy.

6 He who goes out weeping, bearing the seed for sowing, shall come home with shouts of joy, bringing his sheaves with him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 126 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm moves between past restoration — verses 1-3 — and present petition — verse 4 — to covenant promise — verses 5-6. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 126 in full. Verse 5 — those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy — is one of the most cited covenant promises in the Beta Israel tradition, read as the agricultural image of covenant reversal.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 126. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5-6 — those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy. He who goes out weeping, bearing the seed for sowing, shall come home with shouts of joy, bringing his sheaves with him. The same act — going out with seed — is done in tears in verse 6a and in joy in verse 6b. Nothing has changed about the work. The seed is the same seed. The field is the same field. What has changed is that YHWH has acted between the sowing and the harvest. The tears are real. The harvest is also real. Both belong to the covenant life.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 127 A Song of Ascents — Of Shlomo — Unless YHWH Builds the House — Children Are a Heritage From YHWH

1 Unless YHWH builds the house, those who build it labor in vain. Unless YHWH watches over the city, the watchman stays awake in vain.

2 It is in vain that you rise up early and go late to rest, eating the bread of anxious toil — for he gives sleep to his beloved.

3 Behold, children are a heritage from YHWH, the fruit of the womb a reward.

4 Like arrows in the hand of a warrior are the children of one's youth.

5 Blessed is the man who fills his quiver with them — he shall not be put to shame when he speaks with his enemies in the gate.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 127 is attributed to Shlomo and is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm addresses two spheres of human striving — building and keeping the city, and raising children — and declares both dependent on YHWH. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 127 in full. The declaration that YHWH builds the house and watches the city is read as the covenant principle underlying all human endeavor: apart from YHWH's active involvement, labor produces nothing lasting.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 127. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — unless YHWH builds the house, those who build it labor in vain. The word vain — shav — is the same word used in the third commandment: do not take the name of YHWH your Creator in vain. Labor without YHWH is emptiness. Verse 2 — he gives sleep to his beloved. The beloved — yedido — sleeps because they have entrusted the outcome to YHWH. The anxious person rises early and stays up late because they have not. The capacity to rest is the covenant evidence of trust.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 128 A Song of Ascents — Blessed Is Everyone Who Fears YHWH — You Shall See Yerushalaym's Prosperity

1 Blessed is everyone who fears YHWH, who walks in his ways.

2 You shall eat the fruit of the labor of your hands — you shall be blessed, and it shall be well with you.

3 Your wife will be like a fruitful vine within your house — your children will be like olive shoots around your table.

4 Behold, thus shall the man be blessed who fears YHWH.

5 YHWH bless you from Tziyon — may you see the prosperity of Yerushalaym all the days of your life.

6 May you see your children's children — peace be upon Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 128 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a covenant blessing — the fruit of fearing YHWH visible in the home, the family, and the prosperity of Yerushalaym. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 128 in full. The closing verse — may you see your children's children — peace be upon Yisra'EL — is read as the multigenerational covenant promise: the blessing does not stop with the individual but flows to descendants.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 128. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — your wife will be like a fruitful vine within your house — your children will be like olive shoots around your table. The vine and the olive are the two most covenant-laden agricultural images in the Hebrew texts — both central to the promised land's identity in Devarim 8:8. The fruitfulness of the household is the covenant blessing made concrete and domestic. Verse 6 — peace be upon Yisra'EL. The same closing as Psalm 125:5. The Songs of Ascents repeatedly seal individual and household blessing with this communal covenant declaration.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 129 A Song of Ascents — They Have Afflicted Me From My Youth — YHWH Is Righteous

1 Greatly have they afflicted me from my youth — let Yisra'EL now say —

2 Greatly have they afflicted me from my youth, yet they have not prevailed against me.

3 The plowers plowed upon my back — they made long their furrows.

4 YHWH is righteous — he has cut the cords of the wicked.

5 May all who hate Tziyon be put to shame and turned backward.

6 Let them be like the grass on the housetops, which withers before it grows up,

7 With which the reaper does not fill his hand nor the binder of sheaves his arms,

8 Nor do those who pass by say, The blessing of YHWH be upon you — we bless you in the name of YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 129 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm opens with the same formula as Psalm 124 — let Yisra'EL now say — inviting the congregation to join in the testimony of survival through persecution. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 129 in full. The image of the plowers plowing long furrows on the back of the covenant people — verse 3 — is read as the most visceral description of oppression in the Songs of Ascents.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 129. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — the plowers plowed upon my back — they made long their furrows — YHWH is righteous — he has cut the cords of the wicked. The furrows plowed on the back are the marks of sustained, deep oppression. The response is not the psalmist's resistance but YHWH's righteousness: he cut the cords — the same image as Psalm 124:7, the snare is broken. YHWH acts as the one who severs what binds and breaks what oppresses. Verse 6 — like grass on the housetops, which withers before it grows up. The wicked who hate Tziyon have no root, no soil, no depth — they wither before harvest.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 130 A Song of Ascents — Out of the Depths I Cry to You — With YHWH There Is Steadfast Love and Plentiful Redemption

1 Out of the depths I cry to you, O YHWH.

2 YHWH, hear my voice — let your ears be attentive to the voice of my pleas for mercy.

3 If you, O YHWH, should mark iniquities, YHWH, who could stand?

4 But with you there is forgiveness, that you may be feared.

5 I wait for YHWH, my soul waits, and in his word I hope.

6 My soul waits for YHWH more than watchmen for the morning, more than watchmen for the morning.

7 O Yisra'EL, hope in YHWH — for with YHWH there is steadfast love, and with him is plentiful redemption.

8 And he will redeem Yisra'EL from all his iniquities.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 130 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is one of the seven traditional penitential psalms. The opening — out of the depths — de profundis — has given the psalm its name in the Latin tradition. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 130 in full. The psalm is one of the most used in Beta Israel penitential liturgy — the cry from the depths, the waiting for YHWH, and the declaration of his plentiful redemption.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 130. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — if you, O YHWH, should mark iniquities, YHWH, who could stand? The rhetorical question has only one answer: no one. No human being could stand before YHWH if he kept account of every iniquity. Verse 4 — but with you there is forgiveness, that you may be feared. The forgiveness is the ground of the fear. Precisely because YHWH forgives rather than marks every iniquity, the covenant person can approach him — and approaching him produces the reverence called fear. Verse 6 — more than watchmen for the morning — repeated twice. The repetition is the insistence of longing: more than that, and then again more than that.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 131 A Song of Ascents — Of Dawid — I Have Calmed and Quieted My Soul

1 O YHWH, my heart is not lifted up — my eyes are not raised too high — I do not occupy myself with things too great and too marvelous for me.

2 But I have calmed and quieted my soul, like a weaned child with its mother — like a weaned child is my soul within me.

3 O Yisra'EL, hope in YHWH from this time forth and forevermore.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 131 is the second shortest psalm in the Tehillim — three verses — and is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a covenant statement of humility — the deliberate refusal to occupy the self with what is beyond its reach. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 131 in full. The weaned child with its mother — verse 2 — is read as the image of covenant maturity: the weaned child is no longer nursing, no longer demanding — it rests with the mother simply for the sake of being with her.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 131. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — like a weaned child with its mother — like a weaned child is my soul within me. The weaned child is not nursing — it is not seeking something from the mother. It is simply resting with her. This is the highest image of covenant contentment in the Tehillim: the soul that has stopped demanding things from YHWH and rests in his presence simply because he is there. Three verses. The shortest theological statement of covenant rest in the entire Tehillim.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 132 A Song of Ascents — Remember, O YHWH, in Dawid's Favor — YHWH Has Chosen Tziyon

1 Remember, O YHWH, in Dawid's favor, all the hardships he endured,

2 How he swore to YHWH and vowed to the Mighty One of Yaakov,

3 I will not enter my house or get into my bed,

4 I will not give sleep to my eyes or slumber to my eyelids,

5 Until I find a place for YHWH, a dwelling place for the Mighty One of Yaakov.

6 Behold, we heard of it in Efratah — we found it in the fields of Ya'ar.

7 Let us go to his dwelling place — let us worship at his footstool.

8 Arise, O YHWH, and go to your resting place, you and the ark of your might.

9 Let your priests be clothed with righteousness, and let your saints shout for joy.

10 For the sake of your servant Dawid, do not turn away the face of your anointed.

11 YHWH swore to Dawid a sure oath from which he will not turn back: One of the sons of your body I will set on your throne.

12 If your sons keep my covenant and my testimonies that I shall teach them, their sons also forever shall sit on your throne.

13 For YHWH has chosen Tziyon — he has desired it for his dwelling place.

14 This is my resting place forever — here I will dwell, for I have desired it.

15 I will abundantly bless her provisions — I will satisfy her poor with bread.

16 Her priests I will clothe with salvation, and her saints will shout for joy.

17 There I will make a horn to sprout for Dawid — I have prepared a lamp for my anointed.

18 His enemies I will clothe with shame, but on him his crown will shine.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 132 is the longest of the Songs of Ascents and is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a royal covenant psalm connecting Dawid's vow to find a dwelling for YHWH with YHWH's covenant oath to Dawid. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 132 in full. The declaration that YHWH has chosen Tziyon as his resting place forever — verse 13-14 — is read as the foundational covenant claim undergirding all pilgrimage theology.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 132. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-12 — YHWH swore to Dawid a sure oath: one of the sons of your body I will set on your throne — if your sons keep my covenant and my testimonies. The Davidic covenant is conditional in verse 12 for the continuation of each generation on the throne, but unconditional in verse 11 for the ultimate fulfillment. YHWH's oath to Dawid cannot be annulled — verse 11 — but the sons' enjoyment of the throne is conditional on their covenant faithfulness. Verse 13-14 — YHWH has chosen Tziyon — this is my resting place forever — here I will dwell. YHWH's election of Tziyon parallels his election of Dawid.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 133 A Song of Ascents — Of Dawid — How Good and Pleasant When Brothers Dwell in Unity

1 Behold, how good and pleasant it is when brothers dwell in unity.

2 It is like the precious oil on the head, running down on the beard, on the beard of Aharon, running down on the collar of his robes.

3 It is like the dew of Hermon, which falls on the mountains of Tziyon — for there YHWH has commanded the blessing, life forevermore.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 133 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is three verses comparing the unity of brothers to the anointing oil of Aharon and the dew of Hermon — both images of abundant, flowing blessing. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 133 in full. The psalm is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the covenant declaration of communal solidarity — the unity of the covenant people as the condition for YHWH's commanded blessing.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 133. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — like the precious oil on the head, running down on the beard, on the beard of Aharon, running down on the collar of his robes. The anointing oil that consecrated Aharon as high priest — described in Shemot 29:7 — overflowed so abundantly it ran down his beard and onto his robes. Covenant unity among brothers overflows in the same way — abundantly, beyond what is needed, reaching everything it touches. Verse 3 — for there YHWH has commanded the blessing, life forevermore. The blessing is not requested but commanded. YHWH himself commands the blessing to flow where his people dwell together in unity.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 134 A Song of Ascents — Come, Bless YHWH All You Servants — May YHWH Bless You From Tziyon

1 Come, bless YHWH, all you servants of YHWH, who stand by night in the house of YHWH.

2 Lift up your hands to the holy place and bless YHWH.

3 May YHWH bless you from Tziyon, he who made heaven and earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 134 closes the Songs of Ascents — Psalms 120-134 — and is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is an evening blessing: the nighttime servants in the sanctuary are called to bless YHWH, and the congregation responds with a blessing from Tziyon. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 134 in full. The closing of the Songs of Ascents with this nighttime blessing is read as the covenant community's liturgical seal on the entire pilgrimage journey: arriving, ascending, worshiping, and being blessed before departing.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 134. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 134 closes the Songs of Ascents with three verses that mirror Psalm 121's structure: the pilgrim's cry is answered by YHWH's blessing. Verse 3 — may YHWH bless you from Tziyon, he who made heaven and earth — echoes Psalm 121:2, Psalm 124:8, and Psalm 128:5. The Songs of Ascents form a unified literary and liturgical whole — they begin in exile, ascend through distress and trust, arrive at Tziyon, and close with the same declaration they began with: YHWH made heaven and earth, and from his dwelling in Tziyon he blesses his people.

PSALMS 135 THROUGH 145 — BOOK V CONTINUES

TEHILLIM — PSALM 135 Praise YHWH — Praise the Name of YHWH — YHWH Has Chosen Yaakov — Idols Are Silver and Gold

1 Praise YHWH. Praise the name of YHWH — give praise, O servants of YHWH,

2 Who stand in the house of YHWH, in the courts of the house of our Creator.

3 Praise YHWH, for YHWH is good — sing to his name, for it is pleasant.

4 For YHWH has chosen Yaakov for himself, Yisra'EL as his own possession.

5 For I know that YHWH is great, and that our YHWH is above all gods.

6 Whatever YHWH pleases, he does, in heaven and on earth, in the seas and all deeps.

7 He it is who makes the clouds rise at the end of the earth, who makes lightnings for the rain and brings forth the wind from his storehouses.

8 He it was who struck down the firstborn of Egypt, both of man and of beast.

9 Who in your midst, O Egypt, sent signs and wonders against Pharaoh and all his servants.

10 Who struck down many nations and killed mighty kings,

11 Sikhon, king of the Amorites, and Og, king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Kena'an,

12 And gave their land as a heritage, a heritage to his people Yisra'EL.

13 Your name, O YHWH, endures forever, your renown, O YHWH, throughout all ages.

14 For YHWH will vindicate his people and have compassion on his servants.

15 The idols of the nations are silver and gold, the work of human hands.

16 They have mouths, but do not speak — they have eyes, but do not see.

17 They have ears, but do not hear, nor is there any breath in their mouths.

18 Those who make them become like them, so do all who trust in them.

19 O house of Yisra'EL, bless YHWH — O house of Aharon, bless YHWH.

20 O house of Levi, bless YHWH — you who fear YHWH, bless YHWH.

21 Blessed be YHWH from Tziyon, he who dwells in Yerushalaym. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 135 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a covenant history psalm drawing on passages from Devarim 10, Shemot 7-12, Bamidbar 21, and Psalm 115, assembling them into a new declaration of YHWH's sovereignty and the worthlessness of idols. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 135 in full. The fourfold call to bless YHWH in verses 19-20 — house of Yisra'EL, house of Aharon, house of Levi, those who fear YHWH — is read as the complete covenant community assembled in praise.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 135. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 15-18 repeat almost verbatim the idol polemic of Psalm 115:4-8. The repetition is deliberate — the covenant community returns to the fundamental distinction throughout the Tehillim: YHWH acts, idols cannot. YHWH speaks, idols have mouths but do not speak. YHWH sees, idols have eyes but do not see. Verse 18 — those who make them become like them. The shaper of lifeless images becomes lifeless. The worshiper of the living Creator becomes alive. Verse 6 — whatever YHWH pleases, he does, in heaven and on earth, in the seas and all deeps. Absolute sovereignty — without exception, without limitation, across every domain.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 136 Give Thanks to YHWH — His Steadfast Love Endures Forever — The Great Hallel

1 Give thanks to YHWH, for he is good — for his steadfast love endures forever.

2 Give thanks to the Creator of creators — for his steadfast love endures forever.

3 Give thanks to the Sovereign of sovereigns — for his steadfast love endures forever.

4 To him who alone does great wonders — for his steadfast love endures forever.

5 To him who by understanding made the heavens — for his steadfast love endures forever.

6 To him who spread out the earth above the waters — for his steadfast love endures forever.

7 To him who made the great lights — for his steadfast love endures forever.

8 The sun to rule over the day — for his steadfast love endures forever.

9 The moon and stars to rule over the night — for his steadfast love endures forever.

10 To him who struck down the firstborn of Egypt — for his steadfast love endures forever.

11 And brought Yisra'EL out from among them — for his steadfast love endures forever.

12 With a strong hand and an outstretched arm — for his steadfast love endures forever.

13 To him who divided the Red Sea in two — for his steadfast love endures forever.

14 And made Yisra'EL pass through the midst of it — for his steadfast love endures forever.

15 But overthrew Pharaoh and his host in the Red Sea — for his steadfast love endures forever.

16 To him who led his people through the wilderness — for his steadfast love endures forever.

17 To him who struck down great kings — for his steadfast love endures forever.

18 And killed mighty kings — for his steadfast love endures forever.

19 Sikhon, king of the Amorites — for his steadfast love endures forever.

20 And Og, king of Bashan — for his steadfast love endures forever.

21 And gave their land as a heritage — for his steadfast love endures forever.

22 A heritage to Yisra'EL his servant — for his steadfast love endures forever.

23 It is he who remembered us in our low estate — for his steadfast love endures forever.

24 And rescued us from our foes — for his steadfast love endures forever.

25 He who gives food to all flesh — for his steadfast love endures forever.

26 Give thanks to the Creator of heaven — for his steadfast love endures forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 136 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. Known as the Great Hallel, every verse carries the antiphonal refrain — for his steadfast love endures forever — twenty-six times. The psalm surveys creation and covenant history from creation through the wilderness, each act of YHWH sealed with the declaration of his enduring hesed. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 136 in full. The Great Hallel is central to the Beta Israel Pesach liturgy — the congregation responding to each declaration of YHWH's mighty acts with the covenant refrain: his steadfast love endures forever.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 136. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The refrain — ki le'olam khasdo — for his steadfast love endures forever — sounds twenty-six times in twenty-six verses. Every act of YHWH from creation to covenant to conquest is sealed with this declaration. The repetition is not redundancy — it is covenant insistence. Whatever YHWH does, however distant or recent, however cosmic or personal — his steadfast love endures. Verse 23 — it is he who remembered us in our low estate. In the middle of the historical survey, the congregation interrupts with the most personal declaration: he remembered us when we were brought low. The cosmic Creator who made the sun and moon is the same Creator who noticed the covenant people in their lowest moment.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 137 By the Waters of Bavel — How Can We Sing YHWH's Song in a Foreign Land

1 By the waters of Bavel, there we sat down and wept, when we remembered Tziyon.

2 On the willows there we hung up our lyres.

3 For there our captors required of us songs, and our tormentors, mirth, saying, Sing us one of the songs of Tziyon.

4 How shall we sing YHWH's song in a foreign land?

5 If I forget you, O Yerushalaym, let my right hand forget its skill.

6 Let my tongue stick to the roof of my mouth, if I do not remember you, if I do not set Yerushalaym above my highest joy.

7 Remember, O YHWH, against the Edomites the day of Yerushalaym, how they said, Lay it bare, lay it bare, down to its foundations.

8 O daughter of Bavel, doomed to be destroyed, blessed shall he be who repays you with what you have done to us.

9 Blessed shall he be who takes your little ones and dashes them against the rock.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 137 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is the most emotionally raw in the Tehillim — the voice of Babylonian exile, the lyres hung on willows, the impossible demand to sing YHWH's songs in a foreign land. Verse 9 is the most violent verse in the Tehillim. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 137 in full. The psalm is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the paradigmatic voice of covenant exile — the people of YHWH torn from Tziyon and unable to perform their liturgy in a foreign land.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 137. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — how shall we sing YHWH's song in a foreign land? The songs of Tziyon are covenant songs — they belong to the covenant place. The captors' demand — sing us one of the songs of Tziyon — is a demand to perform covenant worship as entertainment for those who destroyed the covenant community. The refusal to sing is an act of covenant faithfulness. Verse 9 — blessed shall he be who takes your little ones and dashes them against the rock. This verse has disturbed readers for centuries. It is the raw voice of the utterly devastated — expressing the measure-for-measure justice they invoke against those who destroyed their children. The Tehillim preserves this voice without softening it. The psalms do not require the covenant person to be calm.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 138 Of Dawid — I Give You Thanks With My Whole Heart — YHWH Will Fulfill His Purpose for Me

1 I give you thanks, O YHWH, with my whole heart — before the gods I sing your praise.

2 I bow down toward your holy temple and give thanks to your name for your steadfast love and your faithfulness, for you have exalted above all things your name and your word.

3 On the day I called, you answered me — my strength of soul you increased.

4 All the kings of the earth shall give you thanks, O YHWH, for they have heard the words of your mouth.

5 And they shall sing of the ways of YHWH, for great is the glory of YHWH.

6 For though YHWH is high, he regards the lowly, but the haughty he knows from afar.

7 Though I walk in the midst of trouble, you preserve my life — you stretch out your hand against the wrath of my enemies, and your right hand delivers me.

8 YHWH will fulfill his purpose for me — your steadfast love, O YHWH, endures forever. Do not forsake the work of your hands.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 138 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm opens the final Davidic collection in the Tehillim — Psalms 138-145. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 138 in full. Verse 8 — YHWH will fulfill his purpose for me — is read as the covenant confidence that what YHWH has begun in the covenant person's life he will complete.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 138. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — though YHWH is high, he regards the lowly, but the haughty he knows from afar. YHWH's transcendence does not produce distance from the lowly — it produces intimacy with them. The higher YHWH is, the more completely he can see and reach the one who is low. The haughty are known from afar — YHWH does not draw near to them because they do not draw near to him. Verse 8 — do not forsake the work of your hands. The final plea appeals to YHWH's own investment: he made the covenant person, he is the worker, the person is the work of his hands. YHWH does not abandon his own craftsmanship.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 139 Of Dawid — A Psalm — O YHWH, You Have Searched Me and Known Me — Where Can I Go From Your Spirit?

1 O YHWH, you have searched me and known me.

2 You know when I sit down and when I rise up — you discern my thoughts from afar.

3 You search out my path and my lying down and are acquainted with all my ways.

4 Even before a word is on my tongue, behold, O YHWH, you know it altogether.

5 You hem me in, behind and before, and lay your hand upon me.

6 Such knowledge is too wonderful for me — it is high — I cannot attain it.

7 Where shall I go from your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from your presence?

8 If I ascend to heaven, you are there — if I make my bed in Sheol, you are there.

9 If I take the wings of the morning and dwell in the uttermost parts of the sea,

10 Even there your hand shall lead me, and your right hand shall hold me.

11 If I say, Surely the darkness shall cover me, and the light about me be night,

12 Even the darkness is not dark to you — the night is bright as the day, for darkness is as light with you.

13 For you formed my inward parts — you knitted me together in my mother's womb.

14 I praise you, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. Wonderful are your works — my soul knows it very well.

15 My frame was not hidden from you, when I was being made in secret, intricately woven in the depths of the earth.

16 Your eyes saw my unformed substance — in your book were written, every one of them, the days that were formed for me, when as yet there was none of them.

17 How precious to me are your thoughts, O Creator — how vast is the sum of them.

18 If I would count them, they are more than the sand. I awake, and I am still with you.

19 Oh that you would slay the wicked, O Creator — O men of blood, depart from me.

20 They speak against you with malicious intent — your enemies take your name in vain.

21 Do I not hate those who hate you, O YHWH? And do I not loathe those who rise up against you?

22 I hate them with complete hatred — I count them my enemies.

23 Search me, O Creator, and know my heart — try me and know my thoughts.

24 And see if there be any grievous way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 139 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is the Tehillim's most sustained meditation on YHWH's omniscience and omnipresence — the Creator who knows the psalmist completely, from every direction, at every depth, before every word. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 139 in full. The declaration that YHWH formed the inward parts and knitted together in the womb — verse 13 — is read as the foundational statement of human dignity: every person is the direct handiwork of YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 139. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 16 — your eyes saw my unformed substance — in your book were written, every one of them, the days that were formed for me. The heavenly book of the living — the same register that appears in Psalm 69:28, Chanokh 81, and the War Scroll — here contains not a record of deeds but the days of the life itself, written before any of them had come to pass. Verse 7 — where shall I go from your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from your presence? The question is rhetorical: nowhere. Heaven, Sheol, the uttermost parts of the sea — YHWH is already there. His presence is not bounded by space. Verse 23-24 — search me, O Creator, and know my heart. The psalm that opened with YHWH already knowing everything closes with the psalmist inviting the search. The knowledge does not frighten the covenant person — it is the ground of trust.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 140 Of Dawid — A Psalm — Deliver Me, O YHWH, From Evil Men — Guard Me From Violent Men

1 Deliver me, O YHWH, from evil men — preserve me from violent men,

2 Who plan evil things in their heart and stir up wars continually.

3 They make their tongue sharp as a serpent's — under their lips is the venom of asps.

4 Guard me, O YHWH, from the hands of the wicked — preserve me from violent men who have planned to trip up my feet.

5 The arrogant have hidden a trap for me, and with cords they have spread a net — beside the way they have set snares for me.

6 I say to YHWH, You are my Creator — give ear to the voice of my pleas for mercy, O YHWH.

7 O YHWH, my Sovereign, the strength of my salvation, you have covered my head in the day of battle.

8 Grant not, O YHWH, the desires of the wicked — do not further their evil plot, or they will be exalted.

9 As for the head of those who surround me — let the mischief of their lips overwhelm them.

10 Let burning coals fall upon them — let them be cast into fire, into miry pits, no more to rise.

11 Let not the slanderer be established in the land — let evil hunt down the violent man speedily.

12 I know that YHWH will maintain the cause of the afflicted, and will execute justice for the needy.

13 Surely the righteous shall give thanks to your name — the upright shall dwell in your presence.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 140 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a prayer for deliverance from violent and deceitful enemies whose primary weapon is their tongue — sharp as a serpent, with venom of asps. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 140 in full. The closing verse — the upright shall dwell in your presence — is read as the ultimate covenant destination: the prayer for deliverance from enemies is grounded in the covenant goal of dwelling in YHWH's presence.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 140. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — they make their tongue sharp as a serpent's — under their lips is the venom of asps. The tongue as weapon runs throughout the Tehillim — Psalms 5, 12, 55, 57, 64, 109, and here. The covenant community's persistent concern with the destructive power of speech reflects its understanding that words are not merely sounds — they are covenant acts. The serpent's venom kills with its bite. Words spoken with malice kill with the same efficiency. Verse 12 — YHWH will maintain the cause of the afflicted and will execute justice for the needy. The covenant promise that underlies every cry for deliverance.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 141 Of Dawid — A Psalm — Let My Prayer Be Counted as Incense — Set a Guard Over My Mouth

1 O YHWH, I call upon you — hasten to me — give ear to my voice when I call to you.

2 Let my prayer be counted as incense before you, and the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice.

3 Set a guard, O YHWH, over my mouth — keep watch over the door of my lips.

4 Do not let my heart incline to any evil, to busy myself with wicked deeds in company with men who work iniquity, and let me not eat of their delicacies.

5 Let a righteous man strike me — it is a kindness — let him rebuke me — it is oil for my head — let my head not refuse it. Yet my prayer is continually against their evil deeds.

6 When their judges are thrown over the cliff, then they shall hear my words, for they are pleasant.

7 As when one plows and breaks up the earth, so shall our bones be scattered at the mouth of Sheol.

8 But my eyes are toward you, O YHWH my Sovereign — in you I seek refuge — leave me not defenseless.

9 Keep me from the trap that they have laid for me and from the snares of evildoers.

10 Let the wicked fall into their own nets, while I pass by safely.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 141 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. Verse 2 — let my prayer be counted as incense, the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice — is among the most significant verses in the Tehillim for the Qumran community, who understood their prayer and study as the legitimate covenant substitute for the temple sacrifices. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 141 in full. Verse 2 is read as the Beta Israel community's declaration that prayer offered in sincerity is accepted by YHWH as the equivalent of the sacrificial incense.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 141. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — let my prayer be counted as incense before you, and the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice. Prayer replaces incense. Lifted hands replace the evening offering. The Qumran community, separated from the Jerusalem temple, built their entire community theology on this verse and Psalm 40:6-8 and Psalm 51:17: YHWH accepts the sacrifice of praise and prayer when external sacrifice is unavailable. Verse 3 — set a guard, O YHWH, over my mouth — keep watch over the door of my lips. The mouth that offers prayer as incense must be guarded. The same mouth that speaks to YHWH must not speak wickedness.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 142 A Maskil of Dawid — When He Was in the Cave — A Prayer — I Cry to You, YHWH — You Are My Refuge

1 With my voice I cry out to YHWH — with my voice I plead for mercy to YHWH.

2 I pour out my complaint before him — I tell my trouble before him.

3 When my spirit faints within me, you know my way — in the path where I walk they have hidden a trap for me.

4 Look to the right and see — there is none who takes notice of me — no refuge remains to me — no one cares for my soul.

5 I cry to you, O YHWH — I say, You are my refuge, my portion in the land of the living.

6 Attend to my cry, for I am brought very low — deliver me from my persecutors, for they are too strong for me.

7 Bring me out of prison, that I may give thanks to your name. The righteous will surround me, for you will deal bountifully with me.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 142 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The superscription connects the psalm to Dawid's hiding in the cave from Sha'ul — the same setting as Psalm 57. The psalm is entirely in individual lament form: the utterly isolated person with no human helper crying to YHWH alone. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 142 in full. Verse 4 — no one cares for my soul — is read as the most complete expression of human abandonment in the Tehillim, and verse 5's response — you are my refuge — as the covenant answer to that abandonment.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 142. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — look to the right and see — there is none who takes notice of me — no refuge remains to me — no one cares for my soul. In the ancient covenant context, the person at one's right hand was the legal defender and protector. The psalmist looks to the right and finds no one. No advocate, no protector, no one who notices. Verse 5 — I cry to you, O YHWH — I say, You are my refuge, my portion in the land of the living. From absolute human isolation, the turn is absolute: YHWH himself is the refuge when all human refuge has failed. He is also my portion — the same language of Psalm 73:26 and Psalm 16:5.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 143 A Psalm of Dawid — Hear My Prayer, O YHWH — Do Not Enter Into Judgment With Your Servant

1 Hear my prayer, O YHWH — give ear to my pleas for mercy — in your faithfulness answer me, in your righteousness.

2 Enter not into judgment with your servant, for no one living is righteous before you.

3 For the enemy has pursued my soul — he has crushed my life to the ground — he has made me sit in darkness like those long dead.

4 Therefore my spirit faints within me — my heart within me is appalled.

5 I remember the days of old — I meditate on all that you have done — I ponder the work of your hands.

6 I stretch out my hands to you — my soul thirsts for you like a parched land.

7 Answer me quickly, O YHWH — my spirit fails — hide not your face from me, lest I be like those who go down to the pit.

8 Let me hear in the morning of your steadfast love, for in you I trust — make me know the way I should go, for to you I lift up my soul.

9 Deliver me from my enemies, O YHWH — I have fled to you for refuge.

10 Teach me to do your will, for you are my Creator — let your good Spirit lead me on level ground.

11 For your name's sake, O YHWH, preserve my life — in your righteousness bring my soul out of trouble.

12 And in your steadfast love you will cut off my enemies, and you will destroy all the adversaries of my soul, for I am your servant.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 143 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. It is the last of the traditional seven penitential psalms. Verse 2 — enter not into judgment with your servant, for no one living is righteous before you — is one of the foundational statements of covenant humility in the Tehillim. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 143 in full. The psalm is used in the Beta Israel penitential tradition as the concluding prayer of the seven penitential psalms — the final appeal to YHWH's name, steadfast love, and righteousness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 143. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — enter not into judgment with your servant, for no one living is righteous before you. The covenant person does not appeal to their own righteousness — they appeal to YHWH's faithfulness and righteousness — verse 1 — and his steadfast love — verse 8 and 12. The entire psalm is built on this foundation: no human standing before YHWH in judgment can be found righteous by their own merit. Every appeal is to YHWH's own character. Verse 10 — teach me to do your will, for you are my Creator — let your good Spirit lead me on level ground. The closing prayer is not for rescue alone but for covenant formation: teach me, lead me.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 144 Of Dawid — Blessed Be YHWH My Rock — What Is Man That You Care for Him?

1 Blessed be YHWH, my rock, who trains my hands for war, and my fingers for battle.

2 He is my steadfast love and my fortress, my stronghold and my deliverer, my shield and he in whom I take refuge, who subdues peoples under me.

3 O YHWH, what is man that you regard him, or the son of man that you think of him?

4 Man is like a breath — his days are like a passing shadow.

5 Bow your heavens, O YHWH, and come down — touch the mountains so that they smoke.

6 Flash forth the lightning and scatter them — send out your arrows and rout them.

7 Stretch out your hand from on high — rescue me and deliver me from the many waters, from the hand of foreigners,

8 Whose mouths speak lies and whose right hand is a right hand of falsehood.

9 I will sing a new song to you, O Creator — upon a ten-stringed harp I will play to you,

10 Who gives victory to kings, who rescues Dawid his servant from the cruel sword.

11 Rescue me and deliver me from the hand of foreigners, whose mouths speak lies and whose right hand is a right hand of falsehood.

12 May our sons in their youth be like plants full grown — may our daughters be like corner pillars cut for the structure of a palace.

13 May our granaries be full, providing all kinds of produce — may our sheep bring forth thousands and ten thousands in our fields.

14 May our cattle be heavy with young, suffering no mishap or failure in bearing — may there be no cry of distress in our streets.

15 Blessed are the people to whom such blessings fall — blessed are the people whose Creator is YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 144 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm draws heavily on Psalm 18 and Psalm 8, combining the warrior king's battle prayer with the meditation on human transience and closing with the vision of a blessed people. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 144 in full. The closing verse — blessed are the people whose Creator is YHWH — is read as the definitive statement of covenant identity: the ultimate blessing is not agricultural prosperity but covenant relationship with YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 144. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — O YHWH, what is man that you regard him, or the son of man that you think of him? Man is like a breath — his days are like a passing shadow. Nearly identical to Psalm 8:4-5, but with a different answer. Psalm 8 answers: yet you have crowned him with glory. Psalm 144 answers: yet you train his hands for war and deliver him — you are his fortress. The human transience is the same. The answer in both cases is YHWH's extraordinary attention to what is brief and fragile. Verse 15 — blessed are the people whose Creator is YHWH. The entire vision of prosperity in verses 12-14 is subordinated to this: the ultimate blessing is not the full granaries but the covenant relationship.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 145 A Psalm of Praise — Of Dawid — I Will Extol You, My Creator — The Acrostic of Praise

1 I will extol you, my Creator and King, and bless your name forever and ever.

2 Every day I will bless you and praise your name forever and ever.

3 Great is YHWH, and greatly to be praised, and his greatness is unsearchable.

4 One generation shall commend your works to another, and shall declare your mighty acts.

5 On the glorious splendor of your majesty, and on your wondrous works, I will meditate.

6 They shall speak of the might of your awesome deeds, and I will declare your greatness.

7 They shall pour forth the fame of your abundant goodness and shall sing aloud of your righteousness.

8 YHWH is gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love.

9 YHWH is good to all, and his mercy is over all that he has made.

10 All your works shall give thanks to you, O YHWH, and all your saints shall bless you.

11 They shall speak of the glory of your kingdom and tell of your power,

12 To make known to the children of man your mighty deeds, and the glorious splendor of your kingdom.

13 Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and your dominion endures throughout all generations. YHWH is faithful in all his words and kind in all his works.

14 YHWH upholds all who are falling and raises up all who are bowed down.

15 The eyes of all look to you, and you give them their food in due season.

16 You open your hand — you satisfy the desire of every living thing.

17 YHWH is righteous in all his ways and kind in all his works.

18 YHWH is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth.

19 He fulfills the desire of those who fear him — he also hears their cry and saves them.

20 YHWH preserves all who love him, but all the wicked he will destroy.

21 My mouth will speak the praise of YHWH, and let all flesh bless his holy name forever and ever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 145 is the only psalm in the entire Tehillim explicitly labeled a tehillah — a praise psalm — in its superscription, giving the whole book its name. It is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11, which notably preserves an additional verse after verse 13 not in the Masoretic text: YHWH is faithful in all his words and kind in all his works — now included in verse 13 of this compilation based on the DSS reading.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 145 in full. The psalm is the great acrostic of praise — one verse per letter of the aleph-bet — and is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the concentrated summary of YHWH's character and covenant relationship with all creation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The DSS Cave 11 manuscript preserves an additional verse after verse 13 not found in the Masoretic text: YHWH is faithful in all his words and kind in all his works. This verse completes the nun stanza of the acrostic which is missing in the Masoretic tradition. This compilation follows the DSS reading — the verse is included as part of verse 13.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 145 is the Tehillim's definition of praise. Verse 8 — YHWH is gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love — the Sinai self-declaration of Shemot 34:6 which echoes throughout the entire Tehillim, quoted here in its context of pure praise. Verse 16 — you open your hand — you satisfy the desire of every living thing. The open hand of YHWH is the image of covenant generosity: not measured, not calculated, not proportioned — open, giving to every living thing according to its need. Psalm 145 is the acrostic of YHWH's character from Aleph to Tav.

THE FIVE HALLELUJAH PSALMS — PSALMS 146 THROUGH 150

TEHILLIM — PSALM 146 Praise YHWH — Do Not Put Your Trust in Princes — YHWH Lifts Up Those Who Are Bowed Down

1 Praise YHWH. Praise YHWH, O my soul.

2 I will praise YHWH as long as I live — I will sing praises to my Creator while I have my being.

3 Put not your trust in princes, in a son of man, in whom there is no salvation.

4 When his breath departs, he returns to the earth — on that very day his plans perish.

5 Blessed is he whose help is the Creator of Yaakov, whose hope is in YHWH his Creator,

6 Who made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them, who keeps faith forever,

7 Who executes justice for the oppressed, who gives food to the hungry. YHWH sets the prisoners free.

8 YHWH opens the eyes of the blind. YHWH lifts up those who are bowed down. YHWH loves the righteous.

9 YHWH watches over the sojourners — he upholds the widow and the fatherless, but the way of the wicked he brings to ruin.

10 YHWH will reign forever, your Creator, O Tziyon, to all generations. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 146 opens the five Hallelujah psalms that close the Tehillim — Psalms 146-150 — each beginning and ending with Praise YHWH. It is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 146 in full. The catalog of YHWH's acts in verses 7-9 — justice for the oppressed, food for the hungry, freedom for prisoners, sight for the blind, lifting of the bowed down, watching over sojourners, upholding widows and orphans — is read as the complete covenant character of YHWH expressed in action.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 146. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — put not your trust in princes, in a son of man, in whom there is no salvation. When his breath departs, he returns to the earth — on that very day his plans perish. The prince dies and his plans die with him. Every human political hope is mortal. Verses 7-9 list what YHWH does permanently: executes justice, gives food, sets free, opens eyes, lifts up, loves, watches, upholds. Verse 10 — YHWH will reign forever, your Creator, O Tziyon, to all generations. Against the mortality of every human ruler, YHWH's reign is eternal.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 147 Praise YHWH — He Heals the Brokenhearted — He Gives Snow Like Wool — He Sends His Word

1 Praise YHWH — for it is good to sing praises to our Creator — for it is pleasant, and a song of praise is fitting.

2 YHWH builds up Yerushalaym — he gathers the outcasts of Yisra'EL.

3 He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds.

4 He determines the number of the stars — he gives to all of them their names.

5 Great is our YHWH, and abundant in power — his understanding is beyond measure.

6 YHWH lifts up the humble — he casts the wicked to the ground.

7 Sing to YHWH with thanksgiving — make melody to our Creator on the lyre.

8 He covers the heavens with clouds — he prepares rain for the earth — he makes grass grow on the hills.

9 He gives to the beasts their food, and to the young ravens that cry.

10 His delight is not in the strength of the horse, nor his pleasure in the legs of a man,

11 But YHWH takes pleasure in those who fear him, in those who hope in his steadfast love.

12 Praise YHWH, O Yerushalaym — praise your Creator, O Tziyon.

13 For he strengthens the bars of your gates — he blesses your children within you.

14 He makes peace in your borders — he fills you with the finest of the wheat.

15 He sends out his command to the earth — his word runs swiftly.

16 He gives snow like wool — he scatters frost like ashes.

17 He hurls down his crystals of ice like crumbs — who can stand before his cold?

18 He sends out his word, and melts them — he makes his wind blow and the waters flow.

19 He declares his word to Yaakov, his statutes and rules to Yisra'EL.

20 He has not dealt thus with any other nation — they do not know his rules. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 147 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm moves between the cosmic — YHWH numbers the stars, sends snow, hurls ice — and the intimate — he heals the brokenhearted, gathers the outcasts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 147 in full. Verse 19-20 — he declares his word to Yaakov, his statutes and rules to Yisra'EL — he has not dealt thus with any other nation — is read as the definitive statement of covenantal particularity: YHWH's Torah was given to Yisra'EL uniquely.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 147. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — he heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds — he determines the number of the stars — he gives to all of them their names. The same Creator who numbers and names the billions of stars bends down to heal the broken heart. The scale of attention is the same: every star known by name, every broken heart known and bound. Verse 15 — he sends out his command to the earth — his word runs swiftly. The creative word of Bereshit 1 — YHWH speaks and it is — is here the governing word of the natural order. Snow and frost and ice all obey his word.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 148 Praise YHWH — Praise Him From the Heavens — Praise Him From the Earth — All Creation Praises

1 Praise YHWH. Praise YHWH from the heavens — praise him in the heights.

2 Praise him, all his angels — praise him, all his hosts.

3 Praise him, sun and moon — praise him, all you shining stars.

4 Praise him, you highest heavens, and you waters above the heavens.

5 Let them praise the name of YHWH — for he commanded and they were created.

6 And he established them forever and ever — he gave a decree, and it shall not pass away.

7 Praise YHWH from the earth, you great sea creatures and all deeps,

8 Fire and hail, snow and mist, stormy wind fulfilling his word,

9 Mountains and all hills, fruit trees and all cedars,

10 Beasts and all livestock, creeping things and flying birds,

11 Kings of the earth and all peoples, princes and all rulers of the earth,

12 Young men and maidens together, old men and children.

13 Let them praise the name of YHWH, for his name alone is exalted — his majesty is above earth and heaven.

14 He has raised up a horn for his people, praise for all his saints, for the people of Yisra'EL who are near to him. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 148 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is the Tehillim's most complete catalog of creation praise — calling every level of the cosmos to praise YHWH, from the highest heavens to the depths of the sea, from angels to sea creatures, from kings to children. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 148 in full. The psalm is read as the covenant vision of universal worship — the entire creation, visible and invisible, joining in the praise of YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 148. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — let them praise the name of YHWH — for he commanded and they were created. Creation praises YHWH simply by existing — by being what he commanded them to be. The sun praises by shining. The sea creature praises by swimming. The stormy wind praises by fulfilling his word — verse 8. Existence itself is worship when it fulfills the purpose of the Creator. Verse 14 — he has raised up a horn for his people, praise for all his saints, for the people of Yisra'EL who are near to him. After the vast cosmic catalog, the psalm closes with the particular covenant: the people of Yisra'EL who are near to him. Nearness to YHWH is the defining mark of the covenant people.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 149 Praise YHWH — Sing to YHWH a New Song — A Two-Edged Sword in Their Hands

1 Praise YHWH. Sing to YHWH a new song, his praise in the assembly of the godly.

2 Let Yisra'EL be glad in his Maker — let the children of Tziyon rejoice in their King.

3 Let them praise his name with dancing, making melody to him with tambourine and lyre.

4 For YHWH takes pleasure in his people — he adorns the humble with salvation.

5 Let the godly exult in glory — let them sing for joy on their beds.

6 Let the high praises of Creator be in their throats and a two-edged sword in their hands,

7 To execute vengeance on the nations and punishments on the peoples,

8 To bind their kings with chains and their nobles with fetters of iron,

9 To execute on them the judgment written — this is honor for all his godly ones. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 149 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm combines liturgical praise — singing, dancing, tambourine and lyre — with the image of covenant judgment: the godly with high praises in their throats and a two-edged sword in their hands. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 149 in full. The psalm is read as the covenant community's dual calling: praise and justice — the same dual calling of the War Scroll, where the Sons of Light fight the final battle with YHWH's praise on their lips.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 149. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — let the high praises of Creator be in their throats and a two-edged sword in their hands. Praise and battle held simultaneously. This is the theology of the War Scroll brought into the Tehillim's closing movement: the covenant community that praises YHWH is also the community charged with covenant justice. Verse 9 — to execute on them the judgment written. The judgment written is the covenant decree — what YHWH has determined and recorded. The godly execute not their own vengeance but YHWH's written decree. This is honor for all his godly ones.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 150 Praise YHWH — Praise Creator in His Sanctuary — Let Everything That Has Breath Praise YHWH

1 Praise YHWH. Praise Creator in his sanctuary — praise him in his mighty heavens.

2 Praise him for his mighty deeds — praise him according to his excellent greatness.

3 Praise him with trumpet sound — praise him with lute and harp.

4 Praise him with tambourine and dance — praise him with strings and pipe.

5 Praise him with sounding cymbals — praise him with loud clashing cymbals.

6 Let everything that has breath praise YHWH. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 150 closes the Tehillim. It is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is pure praise — six verses with no petition, no lament, no historical recitation — only the sustained call for everything with breath to praise YHWH with every instrument available. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 150 in full. The closing psalm of the Tehillim is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the ultimate covenant declaration: after everything — all the lament, the petition, the history, the wisdom — the final word is praise.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 150. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 150 is six verses of nothing but praise. Every verse begins with praise him — halleluhu. The psalm asks no questions, makes no petitions, recalls no history, invokes no covenant promise. It simply calls everything that breathes to praise YHWH with every instrument — trumpet, lute, harp, tambourine, dance, strings, pipe, cymbals. Verse 6 — let everything that has breath praise YHWH. The entire Tehillim — from Psalm 1's blessed is the man who walks in YHWH's Torah through every lament and praise and wisdom psalm — arrives here: let everything that has breath praise YHWH. This is the destination of the covenant life.
